transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi.
Hey, how's it going?
Good, how are you?
Good.
I saw that you were in the Google Doc.
You were leaving some comments.
I'd like to hear some high-level feedback beyond the line edits.
Yeah, so here's what I did.
I shared the article without providing any comments.
Because I want the unvarnished truth with a bunch of people that are experts, right?
Both in the school segment as well as just the general security segment, right?
So I wanted to see their reaction to the content without explaining what was going on.
And if you have access to the document, you see their commentary, right?
Which I think is really interesting and useful.
And some of it doesn't apply because they don't have the full picture of what's happening.
But I was interested in getting their reaction to like, oh, this sucks, or this is poorly written, or this sounds like a robot, or, you know, that type of stuff.
Or this is completely wrong, okay?
And I...
I for the most part, I mean, we are already in a very good spot based on this particular article.
think nobody complained about the tone or how it's written, which normally is an issue with AI.
With one exception, maybe two, nobody objected to the actual content.
So, I think the structure of the document was fine, the tone, where it was going, the use case seems, for the most part, technically accurate.
There's always, like, this mental issue that people need to kind of resolve, which is, like, they always tend to think about just us and what we do well.
Versus what should be, which I think they'll get used to.
I'm not concerned when I will explain the whole picture.
So I think there's a lot of good stuff.
It's not that it's, you know, done and perfect, but I think it's really close.
And the question is, how far do we want to push the technical accuracy?

Not so much the technical accuracy, but there's aspects that we may not necessarily, if we had we written it ourselves, we probably wouldn't have mentioned as a thing, like the comment on the acoustic sensors.
I think as a company, probably our experts would have not recommended acoustic sensors as part of the, as part of the, uh,
A solution because our general stance is we're trying to prevent things.
Acoustic sensors basically are A, not super reliable, B, you know, at that point we haven't done our job because, you know, the gun has been fired.
So now we could argue, does it matter?
One aspect could be maybe there is, and I was going to ask you, how did acoustic sensor came up?
If it's just a model doing its research and finding that there's a lot of conversations around it, therefore it included a comment on it, and it'll help us from an SEO point of view?
Or, yeah, so what's the logic there?
But that's, I think, that's already pretty good.
Like, if that's what we're finding, um
In terms of response, I mean, we're already in good shape.
Yeah, that's great.
I mean, this is something having worked at a content agency before AI, these types of comments even come up with human-generated content.
So, like, what kind of product features do we want to highlight, what we don't want to?

And this is something that we can take your feedback and incorporate it back into the writing guidelines to make sure that, like, for example, the acoustic sensors aren't brought up, like, the next time around.
But I also see this as, like, there's always going to be some degree of fine-tuning that goes into, like, the final draft.
So, like you said, if this is what we're looking at for the first draft, I'm, like, really happy.
I think I appreciate all of the comments that the team left on the doc.
We usually go through, so we go through all of the comments on the calibration blog, and then we kind of fold them back into the writing guidelines.
So, you know, they can really leave as many comments as they like, because this is, like, that's the point of the calibration blog, really.
Yeah, so I think this is great.
I'm encouraged by the feedback because I think it's easily addressable.
To be honest with you, I myself didn't know about this thing, this point of view that they had on acoustic sensors.
It's probably not the most important one, in my opinion.
I think there are more subtleties that are, like, the most important subtleties that I want to call out are the ones around PII.
So, for example, that question, who is holding the gun, that's a big deal, because in order to say who, you should probably do...
Facial recognition, which, A, we don't want to do, and B, opens up a whole lot of issues in terms of privacy protection.
So we want to promote the use of AI that does not require facial recognition.
And that's kind of like a big philosophical thing that we should be being part of our writing guidelines.
So Jody presented a slightly different question that kind of goes, still fits, but takes away that risk.
I really like this.
like, rephrase it as, is the weapon brandished versus like, who's holding the gun?
Okay, so now you can say whether, you know, someone is holding the gun without needing to know who.
Okay?
So that's, that's one.
That I think applies generally to other pieces that we're going to write, and it's the PII stuff, and it's no facial recognition.

Now, there are solutions out there that do facial recognition, and some customers may want it, but we probably don't want to make it, like, if we do the 10 steps to achieve something, we don't want to make it an ingredient, okay?
I mean, I guess we're also going to be, I don't know, but, like, it seems like we make the point of saying that's not the right approach.
Like, because we have this whole thing of, like, privacy by design.
Yeah, And we kind of take a stance as, like, we preserve people's privacy.
Right.
So identity will never be a thing, or will it just be about the action, but not the identity perpetrator.
Exactly.
So that we need to make sure, so it's well spelled out in our messaging document, because.
I feel like if the model reads from our messaging document correctly, we should be able to avoid it.
Okay.
So that's one thing.
Acoustic sensors, I mean, I just learned something as well.

So you're going to see immediate reaction like it's written there.
Oh, this puts us at a disadvantage if you write it this way.
I'm not like, I think it's more important to, especially in this type of article, to say that, you know, this is a capability that people should be.

The one comment that I agree with is like, if we say that this is what a modern AI platform should do, then we end up, then we overemphasize modern as part of this capability and not other capabilities, then we may out of disqualify ourselves, which obviously would be wrong.

So I would just also kind of, we're going to need to learn a little bit more granularly some of these aspects, but in this particular article, we may want to just tone down that aspect of what maybe we position as a capability or something to think about, additional.
So again, I learned something, another one too.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
But those are the two ones that I think are most important, in my opinion.

mean.

But it works by, we upload prompts, which are prompts that we assume your customers are asking AI platforms.
Because ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, all of those LLMs, they do not share user prompts with anybody.
That's a black box, and it's highly unlikely that they're ever going to do that.
Um, okay.
So what we do is we basically put ourselves in the shoes of our customer's audience, and we're like, okay, what would this persona ask about AI for gun detection, for example?

And then we upload all of those prompts into Scrunch, and then the tool throughout the day, it will ping different LLMs with those questions, and it analyzes the responses, and it returns an AI visibility report, so that you can see not only the prompts where you show up, but also the prompts where your competitors show up.
So we uploaded, I mean, we gave the dashboard a list of your direct competitors, so we can see, like, where your visibility is lower versus higher.
